Mordecai declared his relationship to the Jews. Public disasters that oppress the church of God should affect our hearts more than any private affliction, and it is particularly distressing to cause suffering to others. God will keep those who are exposed to evil because of the tenderness of their consciences.

We tend to avoid services that involve danger or loss. But when the cause of Christ and his people demands it, we must take up our cross and follow him. When Christians are inclined to consider their own comfort or safety rather than the public good, they should be blamed. The law was clear; everyone knew it. It is not so in the court of the King of kings: we may always come boldly to his throne of grace and can be sure of an answer of peace to the prayer of faith. We are welcome even into the holiest through the blood of Jesus. Providence arranged that, at that time, the king’s affections had cooled toward Esther; her faith and courage were therefore more tested; and God's goodness in the favor she now found with the king shone all the brighter. Haman no doubt did what he could to turn the king against her. Mordecai suggested that it was a cause which, one way or another, would certainly be decided, and which therefore she might safely undertake. This was the language of strong faith, which does not waver at a promise when danger is most threatening, but hopes against hope. Whoever by sinful schemes tries to save his life and will not trust God with it in the way of duty will lose it through sin. Divine Providence had regard for this matter in bringing Esther to be queen. Therefore you are obligated in gratitude to do this service for God and his church, otherwise you do not fulfill the purpose of your being raised up. There is wise counsel and design in all the providences of God, which will prove that they are all intended for the good of the church. We should each consider why God has placed us where we are and strive to answer that reason; and be careful that we do not let it pass. Having solemnly committed our souls and our cause to God, we may venture upon his service. All dangers are insignificant compared with the danger of losing our souls. But the trembling sinner is often as afraid of completely trusting in the Lord’s free mercy as Esther was of approaching the king. Let him trust, as she did, with earnest prayer and supplication, and he will fare as well or better than she did. The cause of God must prevail; we are safe in being united to it.
